subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Political Participation of 18–24 Year Olds, 2018 and 2020
stimulus: Source: CIRCLE/Tisch College, 2020. Bar graph showing Political Participation of 18–24 Year Olds in the 2018 and 2020 Election Cycles, comparing various forms of participation (voting, donating money to a campaign, contacting officials, attending a march or demonstration, etc.) between the midterm (2018) and presidential (2020) elections.

parts.prompt: Identify the form of political participation that saw the largest change between the 2018 and 2020 elections, as shown in the bar graph.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Identifies donating money to a campaign as the form of political participation with the largest change between 2018 and 2020.
parts.scoring_notes: Only 'donated money to a campaign' earns the point.

parts.prompt: Describe the difference in overall participation between the midterm and presidential elections, as shown in the bar graph.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Describes that there was more political participation in the presidential election (2020) than in the midterm election (2018). Accept any accurate description that captures the overall greater participation in the presidential year across most or all categories.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any accurate description of the overall pattern. The response must compare midterm to presidential election participation, not just describe one.

parts.prompt: Draw a conclusion about how linkage institutions might have contributed to the difference in political participation between the midterm and presidential elections, as shown in the bar graph.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Draws a conclusion about linkage institutions and participation differences. Accept: participation might have been weaker during the midterm because there was less media coverage than in the presidential election; interest groups provided more opportunities for youth involvement during the presidential election; political parties concentrated on getting out the youth vote more in presidential years. NOTE: Acceptable responses do not need to identify a specific type of linkage institution.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must connect a linkage institution to the participation difference — not just describe the difference. Accept any plausible conclusion about how linkage institutions contributed.

parts.prompt: Explain how the different levels of political participation, as shown in the bar graph, might demonstrate the concept of political efficacy.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Explains the connection to political efficacy. Accept: if people believe voting makes a difference, they would be more likely to try to convince others to vote; if people believe midterm elections matter less than presidential elections, they would be less likely to participate during a midterm; if people believe their government responds to citizens, they would be more likely to attend a march or demonstration.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must connect the participation data to the concept of political efficacy (belief that one's participation matters or makes a difference). Accept either internal or external efficacy as a lens.
